elements.sent-2411-001: Interacting or nonlinear lattices can host emergent particle-like modes, such as Bogoliubov quasiparticles, whose band topology and other properties are potentially highly tunable.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite originating in the study of superconducting materials, Bogoliubov quasiparticles can also occur in synthetic metamaterials.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we implement a nonlinear driven-dissipative circuit whose fluctutations are Bogoliubov modes possessing nontrivial non-Hermitian band topology.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show experimentally that the system exhibits a switchable non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE), which abruptly appears when the on-site driving voltage amplitude exceeds a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast to earlier realizations of the NHSE and related phenomena in circuit models, the switchable NHSE in our system occurs in Bogoliubov modes, which are strongly affected by how the system is driven.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, unlike other experimental platforms hosting non-Hermitian Bogoliubov modes, our system does not contain unconventional asymmetric hopping nonlinearities, only a local Kerr-type nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-007: Introduction---Interacting phases of matter can host various types of emergent quasiparticles, which have the potential to exhibit nontrivial band topology.
elements.sent-2411-008: Such quasiparticles, if they can be reliably produced, may have important implications; for instance, Majorana quasiparticles in topological superconductors \cite{alicea2012new, beenakker2013search, sato2016majorana, sato2017topological would enable robust quantum computing \cite{aasen2016milestones, sarma2015majorana .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The realization of topological superconductors is a major ongoing challenge in experimental condensed matter physics \cite{fu2008superconducting, leijnse2012introduction, sato2017topological, zhang2018observation, frolov2020topological, trang2020conversion , with recent progress in identifying them through the proximity effect \cite{fu2008superconducting, frolov2020topological, trang2020conversion .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Another way to explore these physical ideas is to use nonlinear synthetic metamaterials.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) transformation \cite{bogoliubov1947theory,zhu2016bogoliubov , which gives rise to Bogoliubov quasiparticles in topological superconductors, is also applicable to interacting (i.e., nonlinear) bosonic systems.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the BdG Hamiltonian for bosonic systems is not guaranteed to be Hermitian \cite{lieu2018topological, McDonald2018, banerjee2020coupling, xu2021interaction, Wang2022nhse , which implies that the Bogoliubov quasiparticles can have qualitatively different properties, including a broader set of topological phases \cite{bender2007making, nakamura2008condition, kawaguchi2012spinor, kawabata2019symmetry, ashida2020non, bergholtz2021exceptional, Wang2023tutorial .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, in exciton-polariton metamaterials \cite{deng2010rmp, carusotto2013rmp, banerjee2020coupling , it has been predicted \cite{xu2021interaction that Bogoliubov quasiparticles can exhibit the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE), which is related to a form of nontrivial non-Hermitian band topology called point gap winding \cite{hatano1996localization, yao2018edge, bergholtz2021exceptional, zhang2022review .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Such non-Hermitian Bogoliubov modes have only very recently been realised in two experiments, based on optomechanics \cite{slim2024optomechanical and superconducting quantum circuits \cite{busnaina2024quantum , by contrast with the numerous realizations of non-interacting topological phases in linear metamaterials \cite{ozawa2019rmp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Here, we report on the experimental implemention of a nonlinear classical circuit metamaterial hosting Bogoliubov quasiparticles with nontrivial non-Hermitian band topology.
elements.sent-2411-016: We uncover a distinctive phenomenon caused by these nonlinear modes: an actively switchable form of the NHSE whereby the intensity profile in a sideband abruptly becomes localized as the driving amplitude increases above a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our experimental setup is based on an RLC transmission line \cite{albert2015topological,ningyuan2015time,lee2018topolectrical,imhof2018topolectrical,ezawa2019electric,hofmann2019chiral,helbig2020generalized,hofmann2020reciprocal,liu2021non containing varactors (nonlinear capacitors) \cite{hadad2018self,wang2019topologically .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The circuit maps onto a one-dimensional (1D) nonlinear lattice model whose Bogoliubov quasiparticles possess pseudo-Hermiticity \cite{mostafazadeh2002pseudo and non-Hermitian particle-hole symmetry \cite{schnyder2008classification,kawabata2019symmetry .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Whereas there have been numerous experimental studies of linear circuit lattices with various interesting quasiparticles as direct eigenmodes (including non-Hermitian modes exhibiting the NHSE) \cite{luo2018nodal, ezawa2019electric, hofmann2019chiral, ezawa2019electric, helbig2020generalized, liu2020gain, zhang2020non , the Bogoliubov quasiparticles in our circuit arise through nonlinearity, and are observed as sideband modes away from the fundamental driving frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In that model, asymmetric pairing processes are engineered into the inter-site couplings, directly causing the Bogoliubov modes to exhibit the NHSE.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our present circuit, however, contains only on-site nonlinearities of the common Kerr type.
elements.sent-2411-022: The Bogoliubov modes exist as fluctuations on a fundamental mode, and their properties can be abruptly switched by changing how that fundamental mode is driven \cite{banerjee2020coupling, xu2021interaction .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, when the circuit's bulk modes are driven by a low-amplitude voltage, the pseudo-Hermiticity of the Bogoliubov modes is unbroken; but when the amplitude exceeds some threshold, the pseudo-Hermiticity spontaneously breaks \cite{bender2007making,ashida2020non and the Bogoliubov spectrum acquires a point gap \cite{bergholtz2021exceptional , giving rise to an observable skin effect (localization to one side of the lattice).}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The circuit itself does not directly contain asymmetric inter-site hoppings, which are often required in linear systems for the emergence of a point gap and the NHSE \cite{hatano1996localization,yao2018edge,hofmann2020reciprocal,helbig2020generalized,liu2021non ; the asymmetry is imparted by the fundamental drive.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our work thus establishes an interesting way to create and manipulate non-Hermitian, topologically nontrivial Bogoliubov quasiparticles.
elements.sent-2411-026: Toy model---Before presenting the experiment, we illustrate its key features using a toy model.
elements.sent-2411-027: Consider a Gross-Pitaevskii equation for two sites $\sigma \in \{0,1\}$, \begin{equation}i\pdv{\psi_\sigma}{t} = (\varepsilon - i\gamma)\psi_\sigma + J \psi_{\sigma'} + \frac{g}{2}|\psi_\sigma|^2\psi_\sigma + F_p e^{-i\omega_p t + ik_p\sigma}, \label{GP_simple}\end{equation} where $\psi_\sigma$ is the complex amplitude, $\sigma' \neq \sigma$ is the opposite site, $\varepsilon$ and $\gamma$ are the on-site frequency and loss, $J$ is the inter-site hopping, and $g$ is a Kerr coefficient.

elements.sent-2411-028: A coherent drive is applied with amplitude $F_p$, frequency $\omega_p$, and phase difference $k_p$ between the sites.

elements.sent-2411-087: Conclusions---We have experimentally realized a classical nonlinear transmission line that hosts non-Hermitian Bogoliubov quasiparticles.
elements.sent-2411-088: Although there have been numerous demonstrations of tailored quasiparticle modes in linear synthetic metamaterials \cite{carusotto2013rmp, ozawa2019rmp , including circuit lattices \cite{albert2015topological, ningyuan2015time, lee2018topolectrical , Bogoliubov quasiparticles \cite{slim2024optomechanical, busnaina2024quantum are qualitatively different because they are inherently nonlinear.}}}

elements.sent-2411-092: In this section, we provide a more detailed description of the nonlinear toy model described in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-093: This is a tight-binding model with two sites $\sigma \in \{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Each site has the same on-site energy $\varepsilon$, linear loss $-i\gamma$, and Kerr-type nonlinearity $g|\psi_n|^2\psi_n$.
elements.sent-2411-096: A drive $F_p \, e^{-i\omega_p t + i k_p \sigma}$ is applied, with the same amplitude but with a phase shift of $k_p$ between the two sites.

elements.sent-2411-104: The matrix on the left side of \eqref{bdg_eprob is the Bogoliubov Hamiltonian $\mathbf{H}$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: It has the form \begin{equation}H =  H_u H_v -H_v^* -H_u , \label{eq:BdGH} \label{eq:SM minimal Hu} H_u = (\varepsilon - \omega_p + g|\Psi|^2) {I}_{2\times2} + J\sigma_1, H_v = \frac{g |\Psi|^2}{4} \left[ (1 + e^{2ik_p}) I + (1 - e^{2ik_p}) \sigma_3 \right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}H =  H_u H_v -H_v^* -H_u , \label{eq:BdGH} \label{eq:SM minimal Hu} H_u = (\varepsilon - \omega_p + g|\Psi|^2) {I}_{2\times2} + J\sigma_1, H_v = \frac{g |\Psi|^2}{4} \left[ (1 + e^{2ik_p}) I + (1 - e^{2ik_p}) \sigma_3 \right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: Here, $\sigma_i$ denotes the $i$-th Pauli matrix and $I$ is the identity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-107: The Hamiltonian \eqref{eq:BdGH has two notable symmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The first is the non-Hermitian particle-hole (NHPH) symmetry \begin{equation}\{\mathbf{H}, \mathbf{\Gamma}^1\mathbf{K} \} = 0, \label{eq:SM PHS symmetry}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\mathbf{\Gamma}^1 = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{0} \mathbf{I} \mathbf{I} \mathbf{0} \end{pmatrix}\end{equation} and $\mathbf{K}$ is the complex conjugation operator.
elements.sent-2411-109: This symmetry implies that if $\varphi$ is an eigenvector of $\mathbf{H}$ with eigenvalue $\tilde{\omega _f$, then \begin{equation}\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{\Gamma}^1 \mathbf{K}\varphi) = -\mathbf{\Gamma}^1 \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{H}\varphi) = -\Tilde{\omega}_f^*(\mathbf{\Gamma}^1 \mathbf{K}\varphi).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, $\mathbf{\Gamma ^1 \mathbf{K}\varphi$ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue $-\Tilde{\omega _f^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The second symmetry is pseudo-Hermiticity: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\Gamma}^3 \mathbf{H\Gamma}^3 = \mathbf{H}^\dagger,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\mathbf{\Gamma}^3 = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{I} \mathbf{0} \mathbf{0} -\mathbf{I} \end{pmatrix}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: This symmetry implies that if $\varphi$ is a (right) eigenvector of $\mathbf{H}$ with eigenvalue $\tilde{\omega _f$ and the corresponding left eigenvector $\phi$ (i.e.,~right eigenvector of $\mathbf{H ^\dagger$), then \begin{equation}\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{\Gamma}^3 \phi) = \mathbf{\Gamma}^3 \mathbf{H}^\dagger \phi = \Tilde{\omega}_f^*(\mathbf{\Gamma}^3\phi).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Hence, $\mathbf{\Gamma ^3\phi$ is a (right) eigenvector of $\mathbf{H}$ with eigenvalue $\Tilde{\omega _f^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: As noted in the main text, the combination of these two symmetries implies that the eigenvalues form either a complex quadruplet $\{\Tilde{\omega _f, -\Tilde{\omega _f^*, -\Tilde{\omega _f, \Tilde{\omega _f^*\}$ (referred as broken symmetry), or two independent pairs $\{\Tilde{\omega _f, -\Tilde{\omega _f\}$ and $\{\Tilde{\omega _f^\prime, -\Tilde{\omega _f^\prime\}$ which are purely real or purely imaginary (referred as unbroken symmetry), or degenerate zero modes ${\Tilde{\omega _f=\Tilde{\omega _f^\prime=0}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Another important property of the BdG Hamiltonian is the ``non-Hermitian particle/hole pinning'' mentioned in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-116: Using Eq.~\eqref{eq:BdGH , the BdG eigenproblem can be decomposed into \begin{equation}H_u u + H_v v = \tilde{\omega}_f u \label{pin0} -H_v^* u - H_u v = \tilde{\omega}_f v. \label{pin1}\end{equation} By left-multiplying \eqref{pin0 by $\mathbf{u ^\dagger$, and right-multiplying the Hermitian conjugate of \eqref{pin1 by $\mathbf{v}$, we obtain \begin{equation}u^\dagger H_u u + u^\dagger H_v v = \tilde{\omega}_f u^\dagger u \label{pin2} u^\dagger H_v^T v + v^\dagger H_u^\dagger v = - \tilde{\omega}_f^* v^\dagger v. \label{pin3}\end{equation} Using the fact that $\mathbf{H _v$ is symmetric, we can eliminate the term $\mathbf{u ^\dagger \mathbf{H _v \mathbf{v}$ and thereby obtain \begin{equation}\mathbf{u}^\dagger \mathbf{H}_u \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}^\dagger \mathbf{H}_u \mathbf{v} = \tilde{\omega}_f \lVert\mathbf{u}\rVert^2 + \tilde{\omega}_f^* \lVert\mathbf{v}\rVert^2.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-026: \begin{equation}H_u u + H_v v = \tilde{\omega}_f u \label{pin0} -H_v^* u - H_u v = \tilde{\omega}_f v. \label{pin1}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}u^\dagger H_u u + u^\dagger H_v v = \tilde{\omega}_f u^\dagger u \label{pin2} u^\dagger H_v^T v + v^\dagger H_u^\dagger v = - \tilde{\omega}_f^* v^\dagger v. \label{pin3}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-117: The left side of this equation is evidently real.
elements.sent-2411-118: Therefore, the right side gives \begin{equation}\label{eq:u-v ratio} \left(\lVert\mathbf{u}\rVert^2 - \lVert\mathbf{v}\rVert^2\right) \Im(\omega_f) = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: When pseudo-Hermiticity is unbroken, $\Im(\omega_f) = 0$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:u-v ratio is satisfied automatically.}
elements.sent-2411-120: On the other hand, for an eigenstate that breaks pseudo-Hermiticity, $\Im(\omega_f) \ne 0$, it must be that $\lVert\mathbf{u}\rVert^2 = \lVert\mathbf{v}\rVert^2$.
elements.sent-2411-121: In the main text, this non-Hermitian particle/hole pinning provides an additional experimental signature for the breaking of pseudo-Hermiticity.
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that the pinning only concerns the total particle/hole populations, which are summed over all lattice sites; the local quantities $|u_n|^2$ and $|v_n|^2$ need not be equal.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{circuitikz}[scale=0.9] \draw (1,8) to[vsourcesin, l=$V_s$] (5,8) node[ground]{} (1,8) to[cute inductor, l=$L_s$, i=$i_s$] (1,5) (1,5) -- (1,4) (-1,5) to[short, i=$i_{n-1}$, *-*] (1,5) node[below right]{$V_n$} (1,5) to[cute inductor, *-*, l=$L_b$, i=$i_{n}$] (6,5) node[right]{$V_{n+1}$} (-1,0) to[resistor, l=$R$, i=$i_R$] (-1,4) (1,0) to[cute inductor, l=$L$, i=$i_L$] (1,4) (-1,4) -- (3,4) (3,0) to[capacitor, l_=$C_{NL}(V)$, i_=$i_C$] (3,4) (3,0) -- (-1,0) (1,0) to (1,-1) node[ground]{}; \end{circuitikz} \caption[Unit cell]{The unit cell of a nonlinear capacitor circuit that can cause Bogoliubov fluctuations.} \label{fig:nonlinear C}
elements.sent-2411-123: Fig.~\ref{fig:nonlinear C depicts one unit cell of the nonlinear 1D circuit lattice (nonlinear transmission line).}
elements.sent-2411-124: It consists of a driven RLC resonator containing a nonlinear capacitance modelled by \begin{equation}C_{NL}(V) = \frac{dQ}{dV} = C_0 + C_1 V^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-125: By Kirchhoff's law of current conservation, the various AC currents in the unit cell obey \begin{equation}i_{n-1} + i_s + i_R + i_L + i_C - i_n = 0. \label{eq:current conservation}\end{equation} The time derivatives of the current through the inductors obey \begin{equation}\Dot{i}_s = \frac{1}{L_s}(V_s - V_n), \Dot{i}_L = -\frac{1}{L}V_n, \Dot{i}_{n} = \frac{1}{L_b}(V_n - V_{n+1}), \Dot{i}_{n-1} = \frac{1}{L_b}(V_{n-1} - V_n).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}i_{n-1} + i_s + i_R + i_L + i_C - i_n = 0. \label{eq:current conservation}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: Similarly, the time derivative of the current through the nonlinear capacitor obeys \begin{equation}Q_C \int_0^{V_n} C_{NL}\,dV, \Rightarrow \quad i_C = \Dot{V_n} \cdot C_{NL} = C_0\Dot{V}_n + C_1\Dot{V_n}V_n^2, \Rightarrow \quad \Dot{i}_C = C_0\Ddot{V_n} + C_1\Ddot{V}_nV_n^2 + 2C_1\Dot{V_n}^2V_n.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}Q_C \int_0^{V_n} C_{NL}\,dV, \Rightarrow \quad i_C = \Dot{V_n} \cdot C_{NL} = C_0\Dot{V}_n + C_1\Dot{V_n}V_n^2, \Rightarrow \quad \Dot{i}_C = C_0\Ddot{V_n} + C_1\Ddot{V}_nV_n^2 + 2C_1\Dot{V_n}^2V_n.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-128: Combining all the above equations, we arrive at the equation of motion \begin{equation}C_0\Ddot{V}_n + \frac{\Dot{V}_n}{R} + \left(\frac{1}{L_s} + \frac{2}{L_b} + \frac{1}{L} \right)V_n + C_1\Ddot{V}_nV_n^2 + 2C_1\Dot{V}_n^2V_n + \frac{2}{L_b}\Bar{V}_n + \frac{V_s}{L_s} = 0, \label{eq:SM EOM}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Bar{V}_n \equiv \frac{1}{2}(V_{n+1} + V_{n-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-129: Next, we wish to show that this equation of motion can be analyzed using a BdG transformation, similar to the preceding toy model.
elements.sent-2411-130: Let us assume the driving voltage has the form \begin{equation}V_s = \frac{1}{2}\left(Ve^{-i\omega_p t} + \mathrm{c.c.}\right),\end{equation} where $\mathrm{c.c.}$~stands for ``complex conjugate''.
elements.sent-2411-131: We take the following ansatz: \begin{equation}V_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right), \Rightarrow \quad \dot{V}_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(-i\omega_p\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + \Dot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right), \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{V}_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(-\omega_p^2\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} - 2i\omega_p\Dot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + \Ddot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-038: \begin{equation}V_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right), \Rightarrow \quad \dot{V}_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(-i\omega_p\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + \Dot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right), \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{V}_n = \frac{1}{2}\left(-\omega_p^2\Psi_n e^{-i\omega_p t} - 2i\omega_p\Dot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + \Ddot{\Psi}_n e^{-i\omega_p t} + c.c.\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-132: We now make two simplifying approximations:.
elements.sent-2411-133: We assume the nonlinearity in the circuit is weak, and that a small perturbation is applied after the system has reached its steady state, producing a small fluctuation on top of the steady state with frequency much lower than the driving frequency.
elements.sent-2411-134: This is the slowly-varying envelope approximation (SVEA) \cite{hadad2018self .}
elements.sent-2411-135: Hence, we drop terms involving $\Ddot{\Psi _n$, $|\Dot{\Psi _n|^2$, $\Dot{\Psi _n^2$, etc..}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: We keep only terms with angular frequencies around $\omega_p$, as higher-frequency oscillations average to zero.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, we have to be careful about how terms are dropped.
elements.sent-2411-138: Terms that oscillate as $e^{2i\omega_p t}$ or $e^{-2i\omega_p t}$ can only be discarded at the final step, but not at intemediate steps, since the nonlinearity can allow them to combine with their complex conjugate counterparts to produce lower-frequency terms.
elements.sent-2411-139: With these two approximations, the two nonlinear terms in Eq.~\eqref{eq:SM EOM become \begin{equation}\Ddot{V}_n V_n^2 + 2\Dot{V}_n^2V_n \approx \frac{1}{8}\left[- \left(\omega_p^2|\Psi_n|^2\Psi_n + 2i\omega_p \Dot{\Psi}_n^* \Psi_n^2 + 4i\omega_p |\Psi_n|^2\Dot{\Psi}_n\right) e^{-i\omega_p t} + \mathrm{c.c.}\right]. \label{eq:nonlinearity}\end{equation} Note the presence of the term $\dot{\Psi _n^*$, which involves the complex conjugate of $V_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: Substituting this back into \eqref{eq:EOM and collecting terms appropriately, we obtain \begin{equation}\left(-2i\omega_p C_0 + \frac{1}{R}\right) \Dot{\Psi}_n + \left[C_0(\omega_0^2 - \omega_p^2) + \frac{i\omega_p}{R}\right]\Psi_n - \frac{1}{4}C_1\omega_p^2|\Psi_n|^2\Psi_n - \frac{1}{2}iC_1\omega_p \Dot{\Psi}_n^*\Psi_n^2 - iC_1\omega_p |\Psi_n|^2\Dot{\Psi}_n + \frac{2}{L_b}\Bar{\Psi}_n + \frac{V}{L_s} = 0, \label{eq:complex EOM}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{C_0}\left(\frac{1}{L_s} + \frac{2}{L_b} + \frac{1}{L}\right)}\end{equation} is the natural frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-141: We are now ready to apply the Bogoliubov ansatz.
elements.sent-2411-142: The procedure is similar to the toy model described in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-143: Let us define \begin{equation}\Psi_n = \psi_n + u_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}, \label{eq:ansatz} \Rightarrow \quad \Dot{\Psi}_n = -i\omega_fu_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + i\omega_f^*v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}. \label{eq:ansatz_2}\end{equation} Here, the Bogoliubov fluctuations are described by the envelope functions $u_ne^{-i\omega_f t}$ and $v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}$, whereas $\psi_n$ represents the steady-state amplitude.
elements.formula-2411-042: \begin{equation}\Psi_n = \psi_n + u_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}, \label{eq:ansatz} \Rightarrow \quad \Dot{\Psi}_n = -i\omega_fu_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + i\omega_f^*v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}. \label{eq:ansatz_2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-144: For self-consistency, the amplitude and frequency of the fluctuations should be much less than the steady amplitude and driving frequency, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-145: Accordingly, we discard higher-order terms like $|u_n|^2$ and $|v_n|^2$ , resulting in \begin{equation}|\Psi_n|^2\Psi_n \approx |\psi_n|^2\psi_n + (\psi_n^2v_n + 2|\psi_n|^2u_n)e^{-i\omega_f t} + (\psi_n^2u_n^* + 2|\psi_n|^2v_n^*)e^{i\omega_f^* t}, \Psi_n^2\Dot{\Psi}_n^* \approx -i\omega_f \psi_n^2v_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + i\omega_f^* \psi_n^2u_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}, |\Psi_n|^2\Dot{\Psi}_n \approx -i\omega_f |\psi_n|^2u_ne^{-i\omega_f t} + i\omega_f^* |\psi_n|^2v_n^*e^{i\omega_f^* t}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-174: Here, we implement a nonlinear driven-dissipative circuit whose fluctuations are Bogoliubov modes possessing nontrivial non-Hermitian band topology.
elements.sent-2411-175: Moreover, unlike other experimental platforms hosting non-Hermitian Bogoliubov modes, our system does not rely on $p$-wave-like pairing interactions, only local nonlinearities.

elements.sent-2411-177: Such quasiparticles, if they can be reliably produced, may have important implications; for instance, Majorana quasiparticles in topological superconductors \cite{alicea2012new, beenakker2013search, sato2016majorana, sato2017topological could enable robust quantum computing \cite{aasen2016milestones, sarma2015majorana .}}
elements.sent-2411-178: The realization of topological superconductors is a major ongoing challenge \cite{fu2008superconducting, leijnse2012introduction, sato2017topological, zhang2018observation, frolov2020topological, trang2020conversion , but some of the underlying ideas can be explored using nonlinear synthetic metamaterials \cite{ozawa2019rmp .}}
elements.sent-2411-179: The Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) transformation \cite{bogoliubov1947theory,zhu2016bogoliubov , the mechanism giving rise to Bogoliubov quasiparticles in topological superconductors, is also applicable to interacting bosonic systems; however, the BdG Hamiltonian for bosonic systems is not guaranteed to be Hermitian \cite{lieu2018topological, McDonald2018, banerjee2020coupling, xu2021interaction, Wang2022nhse , so the Bogoliubov quasiparticles can have qualitatively different properties, including a broader set of topological phases \cite{bender2007making, nakamura2008condition, kawaguchi2012spinor, kawabata2019symmetry, ashida2020non, bergholtz2021exceptional, Wang2023tutorial .}}}

elements.sent-2411-181: This behavior has recently been demonstrated in experiments on optomechanics \cite{slim2024optomechanical and superconducting quantum circuits \cite{busnaina2024quantum .}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Here, we experimentally realize NH Bogoliubov quasiparticles using a nonlinear electric circuit, and uncover an actively-switchable form of the NHSE different from the fixed skin effects of earlier models.
elements.sent-2411-183: When the amplitude of a driving field increases above a certain threshold, the Bogoliubov modes abruptly switch from trivial to nontrivial point gap topology, resulting in a sudden onset of localization in a sideband.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our experiment uses an RLC transmission line \cite{albert2015topological,ningyuan2015time,lee2018topolectrical,imhof2018topolectrical,ezawa2019electric,hofmann2019chiral,helbig2020generalized,hofmann2020reciprocal,liu2021non containing varactors (nonlinear capacitors) \cite{hadad2018self,wang2019topologically , which maps onto a one-dimensional nonlinear lattice hosting NH Bogoliubov quasiparticles.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: While linear circuits have been extensively used to study the NHSE and other aspects of band topology \cite{luo2018nodal, ezawa2019electric, hofmann2019chiral, ezawa2019electric, helbig2020generalized, liu2020gain, zhang2020non , the switchable NHSE explored in this work relies on both non-Hermiticity and nonlinearity, with the Bogoliubov quasiparticles manifesting as sideband modes away from the fundamental driving frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-186: We note that there have been previous experiments on nonlinear circuit lattices focusing on quasiparticles produced via higher-harmonic generation, which is distinct from the Bogoliubov mechanism \cite{buryak1995solitons, shadrivov2006second, shadrivov2008tunable, Wang2019nonlin, smirnova2019third .}
elements.sent-2411-187: The Bogoliubov quasiparticles in our system are fluctuations on an externally-driven fundamental mode \cite{banerjee2020coupling, xu2021interaction , governed by a NH BdG Hamiltonian.}

elements.sent-2411-259: We have revised the paper to address their comments, and major changes are \textcolor{blue {highlighted in blue in the resubmitted manuscript.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Below, each of the comments from each referee is quoted and followed by our response.
elements.sent-2411-261: We hope the revised manuscript is suitable for publication in Physical Review B as a Letter.
elements.sent-2411-262: Comment---``I am not clear regarding the principal distinction between the current model and the bosonic Kitaev chain (BKC), as asserted by the authors in the Introduction.
elements.sent-2411-263: In the context of the BKC, the pairing process, which can be alternatively described as the two-mode squeezing or single-mode squeezing interaction, is employed to introduce the non-Hermiticity of the BdG Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-264: Notably, this pairing process is symmetric, given that the original system Hamiltonian is Hermitian.
elements.sent-2411-265: It would be beneficial if the authors could present a further clarification of the differences.'' Response---Although our system and the BKC both involve BdG transformations and non-Hermitian band topology, they are fundamentally different models, with different assumptions and exhibiting different physical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-266: The BKC relies on a bosonic lattice with ``p-wave'' inter-site interactions, of the form \begin{equation}a_j^\dagger a_{j+1}^\dagger + \text{h.c.}\end{equation} In the original fermionic Kitaev model, this exotic pairing induces nonlocal correlations (Cooper pairs ``shared'' between sites), leading to the famous Majorana zero modes.
elements.sent-2411-267: The BKC model translates this into a nonlocal nonlinearity for bosons.
elements.sent-2411-268: Our model, by contrast, uses a purely local nonlinearity, of the sort commonly used to model Kerr nonlinearities in nonlinear optics.
elements.sent-2411-269: In other words, it lacks the nonlocality that is the key distinguishing feature of the fermionic/bosonic Kitaev models.
elements.sent-2411-270: The mechanism by which the non-Hermitian skin effect arises in our model is also fundamentally different from the BKC.
elements.sent-2411-271: In the BKC, the underyling Hamiltonian is Hermitian, and non-Hermiticity arises after segregating the fundamental modes into two decoupled ``sectors'' that are essentially Hatano-Nelson chains.
elements.sent-2411-272: In our model, the Bogoliubov modes describe fluctuations around a fundamental mode.
elements.sent-2411-273: They are thereby intrinsically non-Hermitian, and effective asymmetric hoppings can be induced by driving the system in a left-right asymmetric manner.
elements.sent-2411-274: This setup allows us to explore the novel phenomenon of a switchable skin effect and point-gap by varying the driving amplitude, a phenomenon that was not found in the earlier BKC studies.
elements.sent-2411-275: In the revised manuscript, we have revised the last paragraph of the introduction with the following passage, to clarify the relationship between our work and the BKC: \textcolor{blue {``In particular, our model differs from the bosonic Kitaev chain [53] implemented in the aforementioned optomechanics and quantum circuit experiments [32,~33], a nonlinear model that relies on a nonlocal $p$-wave pairing interaction to generate effective asymmetric hoppings.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: By contrast, our scheme only requires a local Kerr-like nonlinearity; the NH Bogoliubov quasiparticles occur as sideband modes, and the fundamental drive is used to impart the left-right asymmetry to the BdG Hamiltonian that produces the switchable point gap and NHSE.'' Comment---``As illustrated in Fig.~2(c) and Fig.~3(d), the authors have demonstrated that a fraction of the modes within the spectrum violate the pseudo-Hermiticity.
elements.sent-2411-277: It would be good if they could furnish a theoretical dissection elucidating the underlying cause.'' Response---The fact that pseudo-Hermiticity is spontaneously broken for some eigenstates and not others is consistent with previously-studied multimodal non-Hermitian systems.
elements.sent-2411-278: In particular, in non-Hermitian lattices with conserved quasimomentum $k$ (periodic boundary conditions), it is known that pseudo-Hermiticity (and other non-Hermitian symmetries such as parity-time reversal symmetry) can break over some intervals of $k$ while being unbroken elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-279: An example is shown in Fig.~2(b) of the main text, which thus explains Fig.~2(c); the behavior in Fig.~3(d) follows similarly.
elements.sent-2411-280: For other examples of spontaneous non-Hermitian symmetry breaking over $k$-intervals, see section "Symmetry-protected topological laser" in [Phys.. Rev.
elements.sent-2411-282: An easy way to think of this is that under the full Hamiltonian of the system under PBC, which includes all $k$, can be decomposed into Bloch Hamiltonians $H(k)$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Then $k$ can be regarded as a tuning parameter which can drive the spontaneous breaking of a non-Hermitian symmetry [Adv.. Phys.
elements.sent-2411-284: It would be helpful to showcase the flexibility of the system.'' Response---We thank the referee for this valuable comment.

Response---We take the referee's point about the potential significance of the spatial profile of the excitation, but our conclusions are different.
elements.sent-2411-409: When perturbing a complex system at a given frequency, it is generically possible to excite all resonances matching that frequency, even if the excitation is spatially mismatched with the eigenmodes (though, of course, the degree of mismatch affects the resulting amplitude).
elements.sent-2411-410: When one strikes a bell, for example, the excitation is spatially localized, while the normal mode extends over the entire bell, yet the bell rings.
elements.sent-2411-411: Our experiment is based on applying this idea to a driven nonlinear system, probing BdG modes rather than linear normal modes.
elements.sent-2411-412: Hence, a small auxiliary driving voltage is applied to the sideband with no site-to-site phase shift.
elements.sent-2411-413: The intention is to excite all BdG modes for the corresponding value of $\mathrm{Re (\Omega_f)$, regardless of spatial profile.}
elements.sent-2411-414: From the measured spatial profile, we then infer the properties of the eigenmodes at that frequency.
elements.sent-2411-415: To verify that the auxiliary drive plays this role, we performed SPICE simulations of the circuit for different auxiliary drive phase profiles.
elements.sent-2411-416: The results, shown in Fig.~S6 of the revised Supplemental Materials, show that the sideband voltage amplitude is indeed insensitive to the excitation choice; in all cases, the profile is localized in accordance with the skin effect.
elements.sent-2411-417: In the bell analogy, the observed oscillation matches the spatial profile of the normal mode, not the excitation.
elements.sent-2411-418: Regarding the referee's overall skepticism regarding the BdG quasiparticle interpretation, let us summarize the evidence laid out in our paper:.
elements.sent-2411-419: The threshold $V_{pp}$ calculated theoretically from the BdG theory matches the observed onset of sideband localization;.
elements.sent-2411-420: The range of sideband frequencies where the localization happens matches the point gap of the BdG spectrum under PBC;.
elements.sent-2411-421: The side of the lattice where the localization occurs is consistent with the point gap winding direction;.
elements.sent-2411-422: The sideband voltage profile is insensitive to the spatial profile of the auxiliary drive, consistent with BdG modes being excited, rather than a more complicated non-BdG scenario where the auxiliary excitation strongly affects the response.
elements.sent-2411-423: In our view, the combination of all these behaviors conclusively points to the correctness of the theoretical description based on BdG quasiparticles, the NHSE, and point gap windings.
